candidate_problems.text: Can the conjecture’s central condition—'no equally predictive shortcut representation exists'—be made precise and testable? The main open problem is to characterize when the minimal sufficient predictive state is unique up to invertible relabeling versus when multiple non-isomorphic compressions achieve the same next-token risk. This requires a theory of predictive-state identifiability under observational equivalence, finite data, finite model capacity, and approximate rather than exact sufficiency. Without such a criterion, the iff claim remains underspecified.

What exact relationship holds between domain symmetries/invariants and predictive state representations? The conjecture assumes that if future distributions are partitioned by invariant differences, prediction will recover invariant-aligned latents, but it is unresolved whether predictive necessity yields the invariants themselves, only some quotient of them, or an entangled surrogate representation. A key problem is to determine when symmetry-induced equivalence classes in the world are recoverable from token statistics alone, especially under partial observability and many-to-one observation maps.

How should 'real' or ontology-aligned latents be defined and evaluated when multiple generative models fit the same token stream? The conjecture appeals to real domain invariants, but an unresolved tension is whether predictive success can ever distinguish ontic structure from merely instrumentally useful latent variables. This raises a deeper problem: under what interventions, distribution shifts, or cross-environment generalization tests do latent variables learned by next-token prediction demonstrate contact with stable domain structure rather than dataset-specific predictive bookkeeping?
